one_line_takeaway: A survey bridging software-engineering V&V/T&E with neurosymbolic AI, arguing the interpretable symbolic component can be used to verify and validate the opaque neural component, while cataloguing where current testing methods do and do not transfer.
key_finding: Applicability of existing T&E/V&V methods to neurosymbolic AI depends strongly on which algorithms occupy the symbolic and sub-symbolic slots and how they interact. For the symbolic side, V&V methods largely exist (truth tables/tableaux for propositional logic; KG-validation tools) but can be computationally prohibitive at scale; the authors argue full verification of the symbolic part is often unnecessary when it only plays a supporting role, because the neural component tolerates noise. For the sub-symbolic side, existing DL testing frameworks are frequently reusable (sometimes with modification), but some neurosymbolic designs (e.g., AlphaGo Zero) have no applicable current testing framework. The central positive thesis is that the interpretable symbolic component can be exploited to test, constrain, or verify the opaque sub-symbolic component (e.g., safety shields, decision-tree policy extraction, logical alignment scores), and the authors call for dedicated neurosymbolic testing frameworks.
core_contribution: This is a survey (IEEE Transactions on AI, 2024) that connects two previously disconnected fields: (1) software-engineering verification & validation / testing & evaluation (V&V, T&E) and (2) neurosymbolic AI. Its own contributions, as enumerated in Section I, are: (a) presenting and critically comparing two existing neurosymbolic taxonomies (Kautz's six-type taxonomy and Yu et al.'s three-group taxonomy) rather than proposing a new one; (b) mapping software-engineering V&V concepts onto symbolic AI (propositional logic, first-order logic, knowledge graphs) and sub-symbolic AI (deep learning); (c) surveying existing V&V/testing approaches for each component; (d) analyzing whether those existing methods are applicable to concrete neurosymbolic applications; (e) arguing that the symbolic component of a neurosymbolic system can itself be leveraged to test/verify/validate the opaque sub-symbolic component; and (f) laying out open challenges. The authors state they could find no prior survey covering V&V/testing specifically in the neurosymbolic domain, which is the gap they fill.
threat_model: N/A. This is a reliability/assurance survey (safety, correctness, robustness, interpretability, trustworthiness of AI), not a security paper with an adversary model. Although the paper lists 'Security' among its index terms and discusses adversarial-example generation, it treats adversarial inputs purely as a *testing technique* to probe model robustness (e.g., DeepXplore, DeepHunter, DLFuzz), not as an attacker capability within a defined threat model. No attacker goals, trust boundaries, or attack surface are formalized.
limitations: As a survey it contributes no original empirical evaluation; all quantitative figures are drawn from the cited primary works, not measured by the authors. Coverage is admittedly constrained by the novelty of the term 'neurosymbolic AI' (authors note difficulty finding papers on ACM/IEEE). The taxonomy analysis is qualitative and the authors acknowledge Kautz's categories are hard to disambiguate and some have no real implementations. The V&V-to-logic mapping (validity<->verification, soundness<->validation) is an analogy the authors assert rather than formally justify. No systematic search protocol, inclusion criteria, or PRISMA-style methodology is reported, so it is a narrative rather than systematic review.

datasets_used: No datasets used by the authors (survey). Datasets appear only in surveyed applications: MNIST (used by DeepProbLog's CNN digit-recognition tasks); NELL + NEIL (KG relations + images) and WordNet + ImageNet (two configurations of the zero-shot recognition via KG+GCN application [108]); knowledge bases referenced as validation resources include DBpedia 2016-10, YAGO, ConceptNet, WordNet, GeoNames
quantitative_results: REVEL (Neurosymbolic RL with Formally Verified Exploration, cited work [100]): evaluated on 10 benchmarks; outperforms DDPG in 7 of 10 scenarios; outperforms CPO in only 4 of 10 cases (figures reported from the surveyed paper, not measured by the authors)

DeepProbLog (cited work [116]): three sets totaling six experiments; outperforms the standalone DL model in 5 of 6 experiments (better generalization, lower computational complexity/training time, higher sample efficiency)
Kautz's taxonomy defines 6 neurosymbolic architecture types; Yu et al.'s taxonomy defines 3 groups (learning for reasoning, reasoning for learning, learning-reasoning)
Yu et al. identify 3 symbolic methods most commonly used as the symbolic component: propositional logic, first-order logic, and knowledge graphs
baselines_compared: No baselines run by the authors. Baselines appear inside surveyed results: for REVEL, the comparators are DDPG (Deep Deterministic Policy Gradients) and CPO (Constrained Policy Optimization); for DeepProbLog, the comparator is the standalone deep-learning model without the symbolic component
